data.title: OpenAI considers $100B spend on backup servers as ChatGPT approaches 1B users: report
data.description:
Microsoft-backed (NASDAQ:MSFT) OpenAI continues to see usage surge for its artificial intelligence tools, prompting the company to consider spending up to $100B to rent backup servers from cloud service providers, according to The Information.

The money would be in addition to the $350B OpenAI has already planned to spend on cloud service providers through 2030, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter. OpenAI's ChatGPT is approaching 1B weekly active users. It has experienced a handful of outages over the past year that sometimes lasted for several hours. 

OpenAI relies mostly on Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to power its AI software. Its key cloud providers include Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud (GOOG)(GOOGL), Oracle Cloud (ORCL) and CoreWeave (CRWV). OpenAI expects its revenue to reach $200B by 2030, which is also the same time it projects to start generating cash.

data.title: DraftKings starts the NFL season strong despite the Buffalo Bills' margin-wrecking week 1 comeback
data.description:
DraftKings (DKN) suffered its worst single-game outcome in its history during week one of the NFL season, according to Citizens Equity Research Analyst Jordan Bender. 

The sports betting operator was hit by multiple player props hitting during the Buffalo Bills-Baltimore Ravens game and bettors piling into the Bills moneyline before the game and during the contest when the team was down late by as much as 15 points. Volume on the high-profile game made the results especially critical to DraftKings' (NASDAQ:DKNG) hold rate. 

However, Bender noted that, excluding the Bills comeback game, the first two weeks of NFL action have been in line with DraftKings' (NASDAQ:DKNG) high expectations in terms of both betting volume and hold rate.

On the positive side, DraftKings (DKNG) has also outpaced FanDuel (FLUT) so far this NFL season in terms of app downloads. Netflix's (NFLX) streaming of the Canelo Alvarez–Terence Crawford fight is seen as likely boosting downloads across the industry.

Investors have been eyeing the early hold rates for online sports betting operators after a disappointing NFL season last year when "customer-friendly" game outcomes dented earnings. Notably, NFL favorites won at their highest level in two decades for the 2024 NFL season, which tends to favor the betting public.

Taking the long view, Jefferies analyst David Katz said that while the first two weeks of the NFL season could have gone a little better for the books overall, the firm noted that week two of 2024 was exceptionally strong, and quarter-to-date trends remain favorable for FanDuel (FLUT), DraftKings (DKNG), BetMGM (MGM) (OTCPK:GMVHF), and Rush Street Interactive (RSI).

data.title: SCOTUS seeks Lisa Cook's response in Trump removal bid - report
data.description:
Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts on Thursday asked Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook to respond by Sept. 25 to President Donald Trump's request for an emergency order to oust her, according to a media report on Friday.

Trump moved in late August to fire Biden-appointee Cook, citing mortgage fraud allegations, though she has since served in her role, having participated at this week's policy meeting.

The administration was dealt two losses by lower courts in recent weeks, allowing Cook to remain on the central bank's Board of Governors while her lawsuit against Trump and proceeds.

SCOTUS is considering whether to grant Trump's push to overturn a lower court decision that blocked his attempt to remove Cook and prohibited the Fed from enforcing, Bloomberg reported.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Apple's standard iPhone 17 "no longer means missing out," say reviewers; Air 'a nod to the future'
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone 17 lineup reviewers seemed to agree that choosing the iPhone 17 "no longer means missing out," as the company brought most of the important features over to its standard phone.

The iPhones went live on sale on Friday.

iPhone 17

"Apple has slowly whittled down that list by bringing the most important features over to its standard phone, but the two biggest exclusions have, until now, remained: the always-on display and high-refresh-rate screen," said Jacob Kastrenakes, The Verge’s executive editor.

Kastrenakes said after testing the iPhone 17 he found that the addition of these two features has meaningfully improved the experience of using the base iPhone.

"The iPhone 17 feels faster, easier to use, and more convenient as a result of these upgrades," Kastrenakes added.

"And for the first time in a while, choosing the standard iPhone no longer means missing out," Kastrenakes noted.

The iPhone 17 starts at $799, has a larger screen, 6.3 inches, up from 6.1 on the iPhone 16, with a faster 120-hertz refresh rate. The phone also has a new A19 processor and starts with 256 GB of storage.

"The standard iPhone 17 also brings an improved rear camera and a vastly better selfie camera that will likely be a welcome upgrade for mainstream consumers. But in terms of hardware design, it’s the most familiar-looking of the bunch," said Bloomberg News' Chris Welch and Samantha Kelly while reviewing the phones.

"This is the iPhone most people should get. It has the battery and camera boosts, said Nicole Nguyen from the Wall Street Journal. "It also has a new Pro-level smooth-refreshing screen that can be both brighter and dimmer than the one on last year’s model."

However, Kastrenakes did note that the most obvious thing the iPhone 17 does not get "is the flashy new camera bar design seen on the iPhone 17 Pro and the iPhone Air."

iPhone Air, iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max

The iPhone Air dominated media coverage ahead of the event. The phone has replaced the iPhone Plus segment.

"If the Air is a nod to the future, the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max are a showcase for what Apple can achieve with a thicker, more conventional form factor. Both should be popular choices," said Welch and Kelly.

However, WSJ's Nguyen noted that each model has more battery life than its predecessor with one exception, the iPhone Air.

She reviewed the phones and found that after a day of use (8 a.m. to 10 p.m.) on a mix of 5G and Wi-Fi, the battery percentage left in the phones were the following: iPhone Air: 19%, iPhone 17: 27%, iPhone 17 Pro: 30%, and iPhone 17 Pro Max: 54%.

Nguyen said that in new iPhones, the front camera received a noticeable upgrade. Selfie videos are now stabilized. Even a jog over rocky terrain looked smooth. The 18-megapixel camera takes sharper, higher-resolution selfies, according to Nguyen.

"In the Air’s favor, it has a 6.5-inch screen, compared to the 17’s 6.3-inch, yet is lighter to hold. It also has the A19 Pro chip, rather than the A19 chip. But oddly, this isn’t the same Pro chip that’s in the Pro phone. It has a 6-core CPU with a 5-core GPU. That’s similar to the A19 in the 17. (The Pro model has a 6-core CPU and 6-core GPU), said TechCrunch's Julie Bort.

Separately, TechCrunch's Lauren Forristal said that Apple's move to bring in the iPhone Air appears to be the company's response to the trend of slimmer smartphones, following in the footsteps of other companies like Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Huawei Technologies.

Forristal added that the iPhone Air could potentially outshine the Samsung Galaxy S25 Edge, which measures 5.8 mm thick. "Additionally, it may pave the way for Apple’s long-rumored foldable phone, predicted to launch in September 2026, Forristal noted.

In Cameras, iPhone 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max add a third lens, a telephoto (now also 48 megapixels) for true optical zoom up to 4 times and “optical-quality” up to 8x.

"Compared with the non-Pros, I saw the biggest difference with videos—they’re more stable and they sound better," said WSJ's Nguyen.

The cheapest Pro model is going to cost starting from $1,099, compared to the starting price of $999 for last year's phones, but it is also getting twice as much storage.

For the iPhone 17 Pro Max, WSJ's Nguyen, "I never understood the Max until I tested this one. You never have to worry about the battery draining. The 6.9-inch screen—Apple’s biggest—is so nice to look at."

Bloomberg News' Mark Gurman said that while not a matter of practicality, the Pro versions also offer fresh outer designs that could tempt buyers.

"The iPhone 17 Pro looks entirely different from the back — and let’s not discount how the new orange color might do. People are going to line up for it, I’m sure," said Gurman.

Concluding, Nguyen said that "if your current phone is going strong and compatible with iOS 26, you might not need to upgrade at all." 

"Analysts are pointing to 2026 as a potentially bigger year of updates, with a foldable iPhone at the high end. We might also finally see promised AI upgrades, such as a smarter Siri—the real change we’re all waiting for," Nguyen noted.

data.title: FedEx FQ1 results beat expectations, but guidance raises questions -- analysts
data.description:
Fueled by higher domestic and international priority package yields and cost savings from the company’s DRIVE initiative, FedEx’s (NYSE:FDX)  fiscal first quarter results left a positive impression on Wall Street and drove shares higher in Thursday’s postmarket trading.

The shipping giant reported a 2.8% increase in revenue and 6% improvement in profitability, both of which exceeded Wall Street’s expectations.

“Despite continued pressures on [business-to-business] volumes from persistent weakness in the industrial economy, we’re ultimately constructive that FDX is taking the right actions to fundamentally reduce its cost structure and capital intensity to structurally improve earnings and FCF profile,” Jefferies analyst Stephanie Moore writes in her note to clients Friday morning.

While the results prompted a surge in shares late Thursday, the modest gains in Friday’s trade suggests a more conservative outlook given headwinds from its freight segment and lingering uncertainties related to the global trade environment and consumer demand.

Notably, Moore isolates several assumptions within the company’s guidance to help investors frame their earnings expectations: operating margin below last year’s 7.1%; freight revenue up by just low-single digits and margins down from a year ago; $1B headwind due to the global trade environment including lost revenues; and $160M headwind from the end of the U.S. Postal Service contract.

“When we unpack the guidance and attempt to get the top-line target within the range amid ongoing tariff and de minimis-related headwinds, strong yield/pricing growth plays a large role,” Evercore ISI analyst Jonathan Chappell says, adding that the expectations for margins to be down at both the Federal Express unit and Freight unit led Evercore to lower their FY26 EPS estimate.

“We believe that given uncertainty surrounding global trade (which is anticipated to impact EBIT by $850M over the last 3 quarter of the fiscal year) and structural competitive advancements will continue to limit both EPS growth and multiple expansion for at least the next 6-9 months, and with more downside risk to the new guide than upside,” Chappell adds.

FedEx (NYSE:FDX) executives also acknowledged the headwinds the company faces from global trade and the industrial economy during the company’s earnings call with analysts.

“There will be pressure,” CFO John Dietrich said on the call. “We talking about $1billion of headwind as a result of some of the environmental impacts…and that the $1 billion will be something that will be a challenge for us as we go forward.”

Added CEO Raj Subramaniam, “[the $1 billion] is a big headwind for fiscal year 2026, we’re doing everything in our power to mitigate the costs as we move forward,” noting that the underlying business “is very strong” into 2027 and beyond.

For Morgan Stanley’s Ravi Shanker, the question which stands out is why is FY26 guidance not reflecting the “significant upside” management expects when the upside cycle returns?

“We have a hard time reconciling the $1B trade headwind vs the $300M for direct trade expense in the walk and it appears general inflation may be significantly offsetting any core revenue gains,” he says, questioning how the company plans to grow revenue by 5% in this environment.

“The EBIT walk implies this is coming largely from price despite pricing power not looking stellar since the pandemic,” Shanker adds.

The volatility in FedEx (NYSE:FDX) shares Friday morning suggests investors are also trying to sort out whether FedEx (FDX) can deliver on its ambitious targets. The stock held onto its overnight gains, but surrendered most of those gains in Friday’s premarket.

Currently, FedEx (FDX) shares are up 2% in above-average volume.

data.title: Glencore holding talks for sale of key Congo copper mine - Bloomberg
data.description:
Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) has been holding talks about selling a stake in Kamoto Copper, which controls its biggest African copper mine, Bloomberg reported Friday, in the clearest sign yet that the company is open to relinquishing control of one of its flagship assets.

Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) is said to have stepped up efforts in recent months to find a buyer for the mine and has held talks with potential bidders, including Orion Resource Partners, which reportedly approached the company last year with a proposal to buy the Congo operation through its joint venture with Abu Dhabi's ADQ.

A potential sale has been discussed as part of ongoing talks over a minerals and infrastructure partnership between the governments of the U.S. and Congo, according to the report.

While Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) has not run an official sales process, it has informally let other companies including Rio Tinto (RIO) know that it is open to selling a controlling stake, the report said.

Kamoto Copper, which produced 191,000 tons of copper and 27,000 tons of cobalt in 2024, has struggled to reach full capacity and has been plagued by operational setbacks, a collapse in cobalt prices, and an ongoing dispute with the Congo government over royalties.

The offtake contracts for the mine’s output - which help support Glencore's (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) trading operation - reportedly are a key point of talks with prospective buyers.

data.title: CDC vaccine panel delays vote on newborns’ hepatitis B shot
data.description:
The CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) on Friday postponed a vote on whether to recommend a delay for vaccination against the hepatitis B virus for some newborns.

The 12-member ACIP panel was expected to vote on the decision on Thursday. However, continuing debate over the issue prompted them to reschedule the vote, which could have upended the existing CDC guidelines on childhood vaccinations.

Eleven members of the panel voted to postpone the vote, while Martin Kulldorff, the committee chair, voted no. At issue is the current CDC guidance, which recommends Hep B vaccinations to all newborns regardless of their mother's hepatitis B status.

The panel had been deliberating whether babies whose mothers have tested negative for the hepatitis B virus should not receive the vaccine until at least 1-month-old. Hep B vaccine makers Merck (NYSE:MRK) and Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) were allowed to make short statements during Thursday’s meeting.

At Thursday’s meeting, the panelists voted not to recommend a combined shot called MMRV targeting measles, mumps, rubella, and chickenpox for children under age 4.

CDC advisory committees issue non-binding recommendations. However, the federal agency usually follows them before issuing its final guidelines, based on which health insurers decide which shots should be covered at zero cost for their members.

data.title: MetLife cut to Neutral at Piper as catalysts are well-known, lower rates may boost competition
data.description:
Piper Sandler downgraded MetLife (NYSE:MET) to Neutral from Overweight on Friday on a lack of upcoming catalysts and the prospect for increased competition among asset managers for capital as interest rates decline.

"We believe many of the catalysts are known at this time, such as Chariot Re's capabilities and the breaking out of the financials of MetLife Investment Management with the close of the acquisition of PineBridge," analyst John Barnidge wrote in a note to clients. "With MET having completed a number of risk transfers, we believe these catalysts have come and gone, and are well understood."

Meanwhile, the outlook for lower rates is likely to boost competition in the institutional spread manufacturing landscape, he added. 

Piper's Neutral rating aligns with the SA Quant rating of Hold and diverges from the average Wall Street rating of Buy.

MetLife (NYSE:MET) stock fell 0.5% in Friday morning trading.

data.title: Nasdaq, Russell 2000 scale new record highs as Wall Street heads for strong week
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday was largely higher, with the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) hitting a new record high on a boost from Apple (AAPL). Small-cap stocks grabbed some of the spotlight after their barometer, the Russell 2000 (RTY), also scaled a new all-time peak.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.2% in morning trade. The benchmark gauge was headed for solid weekly gains, as market participants finally received an interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve after nearly nine months of inaction on monetary policy. 

The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was +0.4%, helped by a +1.1% rise in Apple (AAPL). The tech giant's new iPhone 17 became generally available to the public today, with early indicators showing stronger-than-expected demand for the product.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.2%. Meanwhile, the Russell 2000 (RTY) had reversed course, -0.2%. The benchmark for small-cap stocks on Thursday ended a 967-trading-day streak without a new all-time high, its second longest ever, according to Bespoke Investment Group. 

For traders, the focus on Friday was on a scheduled phone call between U.S. President Donald Trump and China's Xi Jinping. The leaders of the world's two biggest economies are expected to discuss a host of topics, including trade, TikTok, and Nvidia (NVDA). 

Over in the fixed-income market, yields stabilized somewhat after a two-day bond selloff drove them higher. Despite the Fed's decision to cut its key policy rate by 25 basis points on Wednesday and a more dovish projection on rates for the rest of the year, the selling was ignited by chair Jerome Powell's more hawkish speech. 

"Both the dots and the statement were more dovish than we expected. However, Chair Powell’s press conference was more hawkish. He downplayed the dots and characterized today’s move as a risk management cut, a characterization which casts doubt on whether today’s move is necessarily the start of a long easing cycle," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said on Wednesday. 

The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 1 basis point to 4.12%, while the more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was little changed at 3.78%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: OpenAI poaches Apple's hardware talent, manufacturing partners for new device: report
data.description:
OpenAI is going after some of Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) hardware designers and even its manufacturing partners, as the ChatGPT maker prepares to launch its own devices, The Information reported. 

The Sam Altman-led OpenAI has hired some of Apple's design, manufacturing and supply chain teams, as well as some of Apple's AI researchers, the news outlet added, citing a review of LinkedIn profiles. 

The company has also signed a deal with Luxshare — which assembles iPhones and AirPods in China — to assemble at least one of OpenAI's future devices, the news outlet added, citing multiple people with knowledge of the matter. OpenAI has also spoken to Goertek, which assembles AirPods, HomePods and Apple Watches, for component supply for future OpenAI devices, the people added. 

Though not much is known about the device, Altman and former Apple exec Jony Ive, who joined OpenAI earlier this year, previewed the device in May with OpenAI employees. One of the products that OpenAI has spoken with its manufacturing partners about resembles a smart speaker, sans a display, The Information reported, citing sources.

OpenAI, which is backed by Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), completed its roughly $6.5B all-stock deal to acquire Ive's hardware startup, io, in July.

Other considerations include glasses, digital voice recorder and wearable pin. However, Altman told the staff that the device is not a pair of glasses, and that Ive had been skeptical about building something to wear on the body.

“We’re not going to ship 100 million devices literally on day one,” Altman told his staff in May. However, he said that OpenAI would ship that large number of high-quality devices “faster than any company has ever shipped 100 million of something new before.” Altman said the aim is to release a device by late next year.

Apple and OpenAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
